Re: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-91-298
Dear Mr. calhoun:
This is in response to your l-etter requesting advice on
behalf of Long Aeach City Councilmenber Douglas S' Drunmond
i"qi.ding the councilmemLer,s respons ibi I ities under the conflict-
"i:i"t"i6.t 
provisions of the Poritical Reforn Act (the "Act")'1
OUESTION
May Councilmetnber Drummond participate in Long Beach city
council consideration of a revision to the transportation element
of the city's generaL plan which would include a grade separation
pioject slited-for an intersection within 3oo feet of property
iwnia ana leased out by counciLne:nber Drummond?
CONCLUS ION
councilmember Drummond nay not participate in.any decisions
concerning the grade separatioir proj-ct if the decision wilI have
i"" fi"u"6ial eifect on the fair-naiket or rental value of the
iEi:.-pi"p".ty of the council'member. The councitnember will also

revision of the tiansportation elernent of the city's general plan
,hi.h u.. critical 
to the' project as a whole and could result in
termination or substantiaL- rnoditication of the grade . separation
project.

considering other components of the revision of the transportation
element of the city's general plan rrhich wilL not affect the grade
separation project, provided the counciLmember has no other
disqualifying interest with respect to the other components.
FACTS
The Long Beach City Council will be considering a
comprehensive revision of the transportation element of the city's
general pIan. The proposed revised elenent applies to traffic
circulation within the entire city and includes over 830 miles of
streets and approxinately 9,000 intersections. 
The city of Long
Beach has a population of approxinately 430,000 persons and covers
a geographic area of roughly 50 square miles.
In the revision of the elernent, the intersection of ocean
Boulevard and Alanitos Avenue has been slated for a grade
separation project. 
You stated the project vould reconstruct the
streets to intersect on two different levels. 
You also stated the
project may require the purchase of real property around the
intersection.
Councilmember Drummond owns a two bedroom, two bath apartnent
at 21 Alamitos Avenue in Long Beach which is Iocated within 300
feet of the intersection of Alamitos Avenue and Ocean Boulevard.
You stated the councilmenber was sole orrner of the apartment, but
shared ownership in cornnon areas of the complex in which the
apartnent exists through a stock cooperative. councilnember
Drurnnond does not reside on the property, but rents it to a
tenant. You stated there is a good likelihood that the proiect
will necessitate the city's purchase of the councilmember's
property .
Councilmember Drunmond has become concerned that his property
interest may preclude his participation in the consideration of
the revision of the transportation element of the ci.ty's general
p1an, and other natters specifically inplernenting the grade
separation project at the intersection of Ocean Boulevard and
Alanitos Avenue.
Econonic Interests
Section 8710o of the Act prohibits any public official 
from
making, participating in naking, or othenrise using his.or her
official 
position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official 
has a financial inteiest. 
A I'public official" 
is
defined in Section 82048 and Regulation 187oo (copy enclosed) as
every natural. person who is a henber, officer, employee, Or.
consirltant of I state or local government agency and would include
a city councilrnember.




Our File No. A-91-298
Page 3
Section 87103 specifies that a public official 
has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a naterial financial effect,
distinguishable fron the effect on the publ j.c aenerally, on the
official 
or a member of his or her immediate family or on:
(b) Any real property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars (S1,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a comnercial J.ending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms availabl.e to the public without regard to
official 
status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars (S2SO) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 nonths prior to the tine when the
decision is made.
Section 87103 (b) and (c) .
You have requested advice concerning potential conflicts of
interest resulting from councilmember Drunmond's interest in real
property and a tenant who leases the real property fron the
counc i lmenber .
Section 87103 (b) provides that an interest in real property
of $1,ooo or more is a potentially disqualifying econornic
interest. 
lloreover, any personz that has made a payment to the
councilrnenber in the past 12 months is a source of income to him
for the purposes of section 87103(c). If the person has been a
source of income to the councilnenber of S25o or nore within the
L2 nonths prior to a decision, that person is a potentially
disqualifying economic interest.
Foreseeabi l itv
As stated above, councilmember Drummond may not participate
in any decision that wil.l. have a reasonably foreseeable rnaterial
financial effect on his economic interest. 
An effect i,s
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
2 Section 82047 provides irpersonrr means an individual,
proprietorship, firn, partnership, joint venture, syndicate,
tusiness trust, comPany, corporation, association, conmittee, and
any other organization or group of persons acting in concert.


Our Fi■e No. A-9■-298
Page 4
]ikelihood that it will occur. certainty is not required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, 
it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 198,
copy enclosed. )
The renovation of the intersection of ocean Boulevard and
Alamitos Avenue would appear to have a foreseeable financial
effect on property near the intersection, as well as the tenant
living on the property. You stated that there is a good
Iikelihood that for the project to go f orr.rard, the councilmember's
property will need to be acquired by the city.
Moreover, it would appear that even absent the uLtinate
acquisition of the councilnember's property, the improvement of
the intersection could foreseeably increase the fair market value
of property near the intersection. 
Conversely, increased traffic
may decrease the value of the property, while the nature and
extent of the effect is uncertain, sone financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable.
uateria l itv
However, the foreseeable effect on the councilmember's
economic interest must also be material to require
disqualifi.cation. 
The commission has adopted dj.ffering gu.idelines
to determine whether an effect is material, depending on the
specific circumstances of each decision. The effect of a decision
on real property in which an official 
has a direct, indirect or
beneficial ownership interest, is material if:
The real property in which the official 
has an
interest, or any part of that rea] property, is
Located within a 3oo foot radius of the boundaries
(or proposed boundaries) of the property which is
the subject of the decision, unless the decj-sion
will have no financial effect upon the of f j'cia1's
reaL property interests.
Regulation ]-8702.3 (a) ( r-) (coPY
enclosed).
You stated that the councilnember's real Property is within
3OO feet of the proposed $rade separation project site. 
Since his
property is in such close proxinity to the property.which is the
sufject-of the decision, the councllmember nay participate- only if
theie wiII be no financial effect on his real property- Thus, if
the decisj.ons will have eJly financial effect on the fair market or
rental value of the real property of the councilmember, he nust
disqualify from participating in the decision.


Our File No. A-9■-298
Page 5
In addition to the financial effects on the counci lme:nber's
real property, the councilmember must also disqualify himself if
the aLcilion will have a :naterial financial effect on his tenant
who is a source of income. According to your facts, the lessee is
not directly involved in any of the proceedings coming before the
city council concerning the revision to the transportation el-ement
of the city's general plan. conseguently, the councilnember need
only look to the indirect financial effect on the lessee.
Regulation La702.6 Provides:
The effect of a decision is rnaterial as to an
individual who is a source of incone or gifts to an
official 
if any of the following applies:
(a) The decision wiII affect the
individual.'s incone, investnents, or other
tangible or intangible assets or Liabilities
(other than real property) by 51,000 or more;
or
(b) The decision will affect the
individual's real property interest in a
manner that is considered rnateriaL under
section La702.3 or section 18702.4.
An interest in real Property is defined to include any
Ieasehold, beneficial or ownership interest or an option to
acquire such an interest if the flir 
rnarket value is $1,000 or
moie, but excludes the interest of a tenant in a periodic tenancy
of one nonth or less. 
(section 82033; Regulation 18233, copy
enclosed. ) consequently, if the tenant leases the property for
greater than a month-to-:nonth basis, Councilnenber Drummond must
ilso disqualify from the decision if there wilL be any effect on
the property interest of the tenant of the councilrnember' In
addiiio;, tire councilmember must disqualify from any decision
which wiIl affect the lessee's incorne, investments, or other
tangible or intangible assets or tiabilj'ties 
by S1,o00 or more'
other Decisions
If you determine that the councilnember $/i1I have a conflict
of interlst with respect to decisions concerning the grade
separation project, the city councilmember wilI also have to
disqualify iitfl re=p"ct to;1I 
necessary decisions that precede
the actuaf grade separation project. 
For example, clecrslons
concerning iunding ot ttre work ire inseparable fron the actual
execution of the project with respect to which the official 
has a


Our Fi■e No. A-9■-298
Page 6
confLict of interest. 
(slreenev Advice Letter, No. A-89-639, copy
enclosed. ) !{oreover, if the decision is one that is critj,cal to
the project and could result in termination or substantial
nodification, the councilmenber must also disqualify hinself.
(Moe Advice Letter, No. A-89-454, copy enclosed.)
Conversely, sometimes large and conplex decisions may be
divided into separate decisions so that a publ-ic official 
with a
disqualifying interest as to one conponent of the decision nay
participate in considering other components provided the other
cornponents are not interrelated to the decision from which the
official 
was disqualified and the official 
has no other
disqualifying interest with respect to the other components.
(Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343, copy enclosed. )
where other subsequent decisions concerning the revi-sion of
the transportation elenent of the city's general plan are
separable from the grade separation project, or where decisions
concern property geographicatly rernote fron the site of the grade
separation project, the following procedure should be followed to
permit the councilmenber to participate:
(1) The decisions for which the councilmernber has a
disqualifying financial interest should be segregated fron the
other decisions.
(2) The decisions from which the councilmenber is
disqualified should be considered first, 
and a final decision
reaahed by the city council lrithout the councilnember
participating in anY waY.
(3) Once a decision has been made on the portions of the
plan ior which the councilmernber has a disqualifying interest, the
Louncilrnember may participate in the deliberations regarding other
portions of the ptln, so Iong as those deliberations do not resul-t
'i., u ."op"t ing oi in any r^,ay affect the decisions from which he
was disqualified. 
(Huffaker Advice Letter, supra. )
Except ions
1. The "PubIic GeneraIlv'r Exception
Ho$/ever, where an official 
has a f inanci.al interest that will
be f inancialily affected by a governmental decision, the official
may still 
participate in the decision if the effect on the
oriicial's- interelt is not distinguishable fron the effect on the
public generally. 
(Section 87103; Regulation 18703, copy
!""io."6. ) For gSg npublic generallyi exception to.app1y, the
decision must affect tne councilnember's interests in


Our Fi■e No. A-9■-298
Page 7
substantialLy the same manner as it will affect a significant
segment of the public in Long Beach. (In re Leqan, (1985) 9 FPPC
Ops. 1, copy enclosed. )
Although we have not been provided with information on the
population distribution of Long Beach, it would not appear that
the segrent of the population of Long Beach within 30o feet of the
site of the grade separation project at ocean Boulevard and
Alamitos Avenue is significantly large to invoke the exception.
2. Participation in the official's 
Private capacitv
Regulation 18700.1.(b) provides that an otherwise disqualified
officiat 
may appear in the same manner as any other melflber of the
general public before his or her agency in the course of its
prescribed governnental function to represent his or her personal
interests in real Property if the property is wholly owned by the
official 
or rnembers of his or her inmediate fanily.
From the facts you have provided it appears that the
councilmember is the sole owner of the proPerty in question, the
apartment. If this is the case, the councilmenber nay appear
before the city council in the sane nanner as any other :nember of
the public, to advocate on behalf of his property interest with
respLct to the decision frorn which he was disqualified. 
However,
his comments nust be Iirnited to his oersonal interests, and the
councilmember should take care to cJ.arify that he is not acting in
any official 
capacity. 
(Larsen Advice Letter, No. A-87-151, copy
enllosed.) The councilnenber still 
would be prohibited from
privately discussing these matters with other nenbers of the city
council or with other city officiaLs.
Blind Trusts
You have asked whether pl.acing the counc.ilnember interest in
the real proPerty in a trust would avoid confLicts of interest in
the future due to the property. Section 87103 provides that an
official 
has a pro-rata interlst in any property or other interest
owned by a trust if the of f ici.al, the official's 
agents, spouse,
and depindent children own dj.rectly, indirectly, or beneficially a
1O-per;ent interest or greater. Thus, placing the . counci Imember' s
property in a trust $/ould not effect the disqualification
obligations of the councilmernber.
However, Regulation 18235 (copy enclosed) provides that
property in a blind trust is not disqualj.fying. Regulation 18235


Our Fi■e No. A-9■-298
Page 8
requires that for a blind trust to be valid it nust cornply with
the following:
(1) The trustee nust be a disinterested party
other than the filer's 
spouse, child, parent,
grandparent, grandchild, brother, sister, parent-
in-Iaw, brother- in- la$r, sister-in-Iaw, aunt, unc1e,
or first cousin or the spouse of any such person;
(2) The trustees must be given cornplete
discretion to rnanage the trust including, but not
Iimited to, the power to dispose of and acquire
trust assets lrithout consulting or notifying the
filer;
(3) The trustee must be required to notify
the filer of the date of disposition and value at
disposition of any original investments or
interests in real property so that information can
be reported on the filer's 
next statement of
Econornic Interests;
(4) The trustee must be prohibited from
disclosing to the filer any information concerning
the replacetnent assets except for infor:nation
required under this subsection or the ninimum tax
inforrnation which Lists onLy the totals of taxable
items frolB the trust and does not describe the
source of any individual iten of incone,' and
(5) If the trust is revoked while the filer
is a public official, 
or if the filer learns of any
replacement assets of the trust, the filer 
must
file an amendnent to the nost recent Statenent of
Econornic Interests disclosing the date of
revocation and the previously unreported Pro rata
share of the trust's interests in real proPerty or
investnents or income deriving fron any such
interests in real property or investments and
disqualify himself or herself, as necessary. For
purposes of this regulation, any replacement assets
of which the filer learns shall. thereafter be
treated as though they were original assets of the
trust.
Ho$rever, even the creation of a bLind trust will not
irnmediately renedy the conflicts of interest that currentLy
confront the councilnernber. original assets and any income
generated from those assets nust continued to be disclosed until-
fhey are disposed of by the trustee. 
(Epstein Advice Letter, A-
A4-224, coPY enclosed. )


Our File No. A-9■
-298
Page 9
Thus, if the councilnember places his property interest rn a
blind trust, he wouJ.d stitl 
be disqualified from participating in
decisions that affect the property until the property is disposed
of by the trustee.3 
lDean Advice-Letter, No. A-88-425, copy
enclosed. ) once the original assets are disposed of by the
trustee, the councilnember will no longer know or have reason to
know which financial interests are involved in a decision, and
therefore nay participate without restriction. 
(section 871oo;
Biddle Advice Letter, No. A-88-403, copy enclosed. )
If you have any further questions regarding this natter,
please feel free to contact ne at (916) 322'59Q1 .

iounciLrnernber uould continue to have a conflict of interest with
respect to decisions that affect the real property.

(slreenev Advice Letter, No. A-89-639, copy
enclosed. ) !{oreover, if the decision is one that is critj,cal to
the project and could result in termination or substantial
nodification, the councilmenber must also disqualify hinself.
(Moe Advice Letter, No. A-89-454, copy enclosed.)
Conversely, sometimes large and conplex decisions may be
divided into separate decisions so that a publ-ic official 
with a
disqualifying interest as to one conponent of the decision nay
participate in considering other components provided the other
cornponents are not interrelated to the decision from which the
official 
was disqualified and the official 
has no other
disqualifying interest with respect to the other components.
(Huffaker Advice Letter, No. A-86-343, copy enclosed. )
where other subsequent decisions concerning the revi-sion of
the transportation elenent of the city's general plan are
separable from the grade separation project, or where decisions
concern property geographicatly rernote fron the site of the grade
separation project, the following procedure should be followed to
permit the councilmenber to participate:
(1) The decisions for which the councilmernber has a
disqualifying financial interest should be segregated fron the
other decisions.
(2) The decisions from which the councilmenber is
disqualified should be considered first, 
and a final decision
reaahed by the city council lrithout the councilnember
participating in anY waY.
(3) Once a decision has been made on the portions of the
plan ior which the councilmernber has a disqualifying interest, the
Louncilrnember may participate in the deliberations regarding other
portions of the ptln, so Iong as those deliberations do not resul-t
'i., u ."op"t ing oi in any r^,ay affect the decisions from which he
was disqualified. 
(Huffaker Advice Letter, supra. )
Except ions
1. The "PubIic GeneraIlv'r Exception
Ho$/ever, where an official 
has a f inanci.al interest that will
be f inancialily affected by a governmental decision, the official
may still 
participate in the decision if the effect on the
oriicial's- interelt is not distinguishable fron the effect on the
public generally. 
(Section 87103; Regulation 18703, copy
!""io."6. ) For gSg npublic generallyi exception to.app1y, the
decision must affect tne councilnember's interests in


Our Fi■e No. A-9■-298
substantialLy the same manner as it will affect a significant
segment of the public in Long Beach. (In re Leqan, (1985) 9 FPPC
Ops. 1, copy enclosed. )
Although we have not been provided with information on the
population distribution of Long Beach, it would not appear that
the segrent of the population of Long Beach within 30o feet of the
site of the grade separation project at ocean Boulevard and
Alamitos Avenue is significantly large to invoke the exception.
2. Participation in the official's 
Private capacitv
Regulation 18700.1.(b) provides that an otherwise disqualified
officiat 
may appear in the same manner as any other melflber of the
general public before his or her agency in the course of its
prescribed governnental function to represent his or her personal
interests in real Property if the property is wholly owned by the
official 
or rnembers of his or her inmediate fanily.
From the facts you have provided it appears that the
councilmember is the sole owner of the proPerty in question, the
apartment. If this is the case, the councilmenber nay appear
before the city council in the sane nanner as any other :nember of
the public, to advocate on behalf of his property interest with
respLct to the decision frorn which he was disqualified. 
However,
his comments nust be Iirnited to his oersonal interests, and the
councilmember should take care to cJ.arify that he is not acting in
any official 
capacity. 
(Larsen Advice Letter, No. A-87-151, copy
enllosed.) The councilnenber still 
would be prohibited from
privately discussing these matters with other nenbers of the city
council or with other city officiaLs.
Blind Trusts
You have asked whether pl.acing the counc.ilnember interest in
the real proPerty in a trust would avoid confLicts of interest in
the future due to the property. Section 87103 provides that an
official 
has a pro-rata interlst in any property or other interest
owned by a trust if the of f ici.al, the official's 
agents, spouse,
and depindent children own dj.rectly, indirectly, or beneficially a
1O-per;ent interest or greater. Thus, placing the . counci Imember' s
property in a trust $/ould not effect the disqualification
obligations of the councilmernber.
However, Regulation 18235 (copy enclosed) provides that
property in a blind trust is not disqualj.fying. Regulation 18235


Our Fi■e No. A-9■-298
requires that for a blind trust to be valid it nust cornply with
the following:
(1) The trustee nust be a disinterested party
other than the filer's 
spouse, child, parent,
grandparent, grandchild, brother, sister, parent-
in-Iaw, brother- in- la$r, sister-in-Iaw, aunt, unc1e,
or first cousin or the spouse of any such person;
(2) The trustees must be given cornplete
discretion to rnanage the trust including, but not
Iimited to, the power to dispose of and acquire
trust assets lrithout consulting or notifying the
filer;
(3) The trustee must be required to notify
the filer of the date of disposition and value at
disposition of any original investments or
interests in real property so that information can
be reported on the filer's 
next statement of
Econornic Interests;
(4) The trustee must be prohibited from
disclosing to the filer any information concerning
the replacetnent assets except for infor:nation
required under this subsection or the ninimum tax
inforrnation which Lists onLy the totals of taxable
items frolB the trust and does not describe the
source of any individual iten of incone,' and
(5) If the trust is revoked while the filer
is a public official, 
or if the filer learns of any
replacement assets of the trust, the filer 
must
file an amendnent to the nost recent Statenent of
Econornic Interests disclosing the date of
revocation and the previously unreported Pro rata
share of the trust's interests in real proPerty or
investnents or income deriving fron any such
interests in real property or investments and
disqualify himself or herself, as necessary. For
purposes of this regulation, any replacement assets
of which the filer learns shall. thereafter be
treated as though they were original assets of the
trust.
Ho$rever, even the creation of a bLind trust will not
irnmediately renedy the conflicts of interest that currentLy
confront the councilnernber. original assets and any income
generated from those assets nust continued to be disclosed until-
fhey are disposed of by the trustee. 
(Epstein Advice Letter, A-
A4-224, coPY enclosed. )


Our File No. A-9■
-298
Thus, if the councilnember places his property interest rn a
blind trust, he wouJ.d stitl 
be disqualified from participating in
decisions that affect the property until the property is disposed
of by the trustee.3 
lDean Advice-Letter, No. A-88-425, copy
enclosed. ) once the original assets are disposed of by the
trustee, the councilnember will no longer know or have reason to
know which financial interests are involved in a decision, and
therefore nay participate without restriction. 
(section 871oo;
Biddle Advice Letter, No. A-88-403, copy enclosed. )

FACTS The Long Beach City Council will be considering a comprehensive revision of the transportation element of the city's general pIan. The proposed revised elenent applies to traffic circulation within the entire city and includes over 830 miles of streets and approxinately 9,000 intersections. The city of Long Beach has a population of approxinately 430,000 persons and covers a geographic area of roughly 50 square miles. In the revision of the elernent, the intersection of ocean Boulevard and Alanitos Avenue has been slated for a grade separation project. You stated the project vould reconstruct the streets to intersect on two different levels. You also stated the project may require the purchase of real property around the intersection. Councilmember Drummond owns a two bedroom, two bath apartnent at 21 Alamitos Avenue in Long Beach which is Iocated within 300 feet of the intersection of Alamitos Avenue and Ocean Boulevard. You stated the councilmenber was sole orrner of the apartment, but shared ownership in cornnon areas of the complex in which the apartnent exists through a stock cooperative. councilnember Drurnnond does not reside
